revision.comment: Bot: add wikidata link templates
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Type of ceremonial dance in Shinto ritual}} {{about|the Japanese dance}} {{Italic title}} [[File:The Kagura Dancer, by Suzuki Harunobu, circa 1766.jpg|thumb|''The Kagura Dancer'', by [[:en:Suzuki Harunobu|Suzuki Harunobu]]. [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{circa|1766}}]] {{Shinto}}
{{Nihongo3|"god-entertainment"|{{ruby|神楽|かぐら}}|'''Kagura'''|lead=yes}} is a type of [[Shinto]] ritual ceremonial dance. The term is a contraction of the phrase {{nihongo3|"seat of god"||kami no kura}}, indicating the presence of gods ({{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}) in the practice.
One major function of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} is {{nihongo3|purifying and shaking the spirit||chinkon}}, involving a procession-trance process. Usually a female shaman will perform the dance and obtain the oracle from the god—in the setting, the dancer herself turns into the god during the performance.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Averbuch|first=Irit|date=1998|title=Shamanic Dance in Japan: The Choreography of Possession in Kagura Performance|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/1178756|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=57|issue=2|pages=293–329|doi=10.2307/1178756|jstor=1178756|issn=0385-2342}}</ref> Once strictly a ceremonial art derived from {{Nihongo3|"oracular divinification"|神懸|kamigakari}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} has evolved in many directions over the span of more than a millennium. Today, it is very much a living tradition, with rituals tied to the rhythms of the agricultural calendar, thriving primarily in parts of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], and urban centers such as [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Petersen |first=David |year=2007 |title=Invitation to Kagura: Hidden Gem of the Traditional Japanese Performing Arts |location=Morrisville |publisher=Lulu Press}}</ref>{{sps|certain=y|reason=vanity publisher, trips edit filter|date=July 2024}}
==Types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Kagura at Festival in Japan (1914 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|thumb|Kagura dancing on a platform, 1914]] There are two major types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} and {{transliteration|ja|odori}}. {{transliteration|ja|Mai}} consists of slow circular movement, stressing quiet and elegance, while {{transliteration|ja|odori}} consists of quick leaping and jumping, stressing activation and energy. The two types can be understood as two phases of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} is a preparation process for trance and {{transliteration|ja|odori}} is the unconscious trance stage.
During {{transliteration|ja|mai}}, the female shaman, surrounded by a group of priests, holds a {{transliteration|ja|[[gohei]]}} (a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify) as well as sound-producing instruments and engages with circling movement to summon deities. Once the female shaman enters a possessed state, she switches into the spontaneous leaping movements of {{transliteration|ja|odori}}.<ref name=":0" />
==History== The epics {{transliteration|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} describe a folktale origin for the dances. In these texts, there is a famous legendary tale about the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who retreated into a cave, bringing darkness and cold to the world. [[Ame-no-Uzume]], goddess of the dawn and of revelry, led the other gods in a wild dance, and persuaded Amaterasu to emerge to see what the ruckus was all about.<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, part 1, page 44f. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Kagura}} is one of a number of rituals and arts said to derive from this event.
Originally called {{Nihongo||神座|kamukura/kamikura}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} began as sacred dances performed at the Imperial court by {{nihongo|shrine maidens||[[miko]]}} who were supposedly descendants of Ame-no-Uzume. During the performance, the shrine maidens usually utilize a channeling device for god such as masks and spears to imitate the trance. With the {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} music that has the power to summon the gods, the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} start to dance to transform themselves into the representation of the gods and receive messages as well as blessings from the deities.<ref name=":0" /> In 1871, Iwami Shinto offices claimed that theatrical {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performed by priests in the west of Japan demeaned their dignity and therefore banned the performance. Owing to the support of civilian performing groups at that time, the performance pieces were still preserved.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Lancashire|first=Terence|date=2001|title="Kagura" - A "Shinto" Dance? Or Perhaps Not|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/834231|journal=Asian Music|volume=33|issue=1|pages=25–59|doi=10.2307/834231|jstor=834231|issn=0044-9202}}</ref>
Over time, however, these {{Nihongo||御神楽|mikagura}} performed within the sacred and private precincts of the Imperial courts, inspired popular ritual dances, called {{Nihongo||里神楽|satokagura}}, which, being popular forms, practiced in villages all around the country, were adapted into various other folk traditions and developed into a number of different forms. Among these are {{transliteration|ja|miko kagura}}, {{transliteration|ja|shishi kagura}}, and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]]-style and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo]]-style {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} dances. Many more variations have developed over the centuries, including some which are fairly new, and most of which have become highly secularized folk traditions.
{{transliteration|ja|Kagura}}, in particular those forms that involve storytelling or reenactment of fables, is also one of the primary influences on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theatre.
<gallery> File:Kagura-Dance-Shinto-1914.png|Kagura dance in 1914 File:Shinto gohei.jpeg|{{transliteration|ja|Gohei}} – a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify File:Kagura villain.jpg|Mask from contemporary {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performance </gallery>
==Imperial {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Nikko_Toshogu_Yomeimon_Kagura_M4482.jpg|thumb|[[Kagura-den]] at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]]] {{Nihongo||御神楽|Mikagura}} is a ritual dance performed at the imperial court and at important [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]: [[:en:Kamo-jinja|Kamo-jinja]] and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]].<ref name="EOS"/> It consists of welcoming, entertaining and greeting the deities with humorous or poetic syllabic songs. Today it is sometimes considered as a sub-genre of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}}, of which it is one of the influences.<ref>{{Cite book| language=en | first1= John | last1=Whitney Hall | first2=Delmer | last2=M. Brown | first3=Marius | last3=B. Jansen |title= The Cambridge History of Japan |volume=1 |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=1993 |pages=650 | isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C | access-date=June 7, 2019 }}</ref> It predated Chinese inspiration, and has indigenous elements as well as influences from other elements such as {{Nihongo||管弦|kangen}}, {{Nihongo||舞楽|[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]}} and {{Nihongo||催馬楽|[[:en:Saibara|Saibara]]}}, which are forms of {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}},<ref name="Tanba">{{cite web |author= Akira Tanba |title= ''Musiques traditionnelles du Japon'' |publisher= Actes Sud |orig-date=First published in 1995. |url=https://www.actes-sud.fr/node/17787}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| author=S. Kishibe |title=''The Traditional Music of Japan, Ongaku no tomo edition'' |date=March 1984 |location=Tokyo |orig-date=1984 |page=42 |publisher=Ongaku No Tomo Sha Corp |url=https://www.amazon.com/Traditional-Music-Japan-Shigeo-Kishibe/dp/B0007B3OFK}}</ref> More simply, {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} can be considered dances accompanied by {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}} music.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://pad.philharmoniedeparis.fr/musiques-du-japon.aspx |title=Musiques du Japon |author=Véronique Brindeau |website=Philharmonie de Paris à la demande |access-date= June 29, 2020 }}.</ref>
The {{transliteration|ja|kagura-uta}} is the sacred vocal repertoire of 26 songs ({{transliteration|ja|Niwabi, Achime, Sakaki, Karakami, Hayakarakami, Komomakura, Sazanami, Senzai, Hayauta, Hoshi, Asakura, Sonokoma}}, etc.) traditionally performed by a male choir for several days, but reduced today to 12 chants performed in six hours.<ref name="Tanba"/> Instruments used include the {{Nihongo||笛|[[:en:Yokobue|Yokobue]]}} and/or the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]]}}, with the possible addition of a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Yamatogoto|Yamatogoto]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shakubyoshi|Shakubyoshi]]}} [[:en:Claves|Claves]].<ref name="EOS"/>
There are several {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} dances, including:<ref name="Tanba"/> * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Japan"|大和舞|Yamato Mai}}, associated with the {{Nihongo3|"song of Japan"|大和歌|Yamato uta}}, making use of the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ryūteki|Ryūteki]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and a pair of {{transliteration|ja|shakubyōshi}}, and with or without a [[:en:Zither|Zither]]; * The {{Nihongo3|"game of Oriental countries"|東遊|Azuma Asobi}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komabue|Komabue]]}}; * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Kume"|久米舞|Kume Mai}}, associated with the {{transliteration|ja|kume uta}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagurabue|Kagurabue]]}}, the {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and the {{transliteration|ja|wagon}}.
The formal Imperial ritual dances are performed in a number of sacred places and on a number of special occasions. At the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries|Imperial Sanctuary]], where the [[:en:Yata no kagami|Yata no kagami]] is kept, they are performed as part of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}} court music. {{transliteration|ja|Mikagura}} are also performed at the Imperial harvest festival and at major shrines such as [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]], [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo]], and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]]. Since around the year 1000, these events have taken place every year.
According to the [[:en:Board of Ceremonies|Board of Ceremonies]] of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} still take place every December in the Imperial Sanctuary and at the Imperial harvest festival ceremonies.
==Folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Susanoo-Orochi.jpg|thumb|[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[:en:Orochi|Orochi]] in Izumo-ryū {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}]] {{transliteration|ja|Satokagura}}, or "normal {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}", is a wide umbrella term containing a great diversity of folk dances derived from the {{nihongo|Imperial ritual dances||mikagura}}, and incorporated with other folk traditions. It is the partial origin of both [[:en:Noh|Noh]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[kyōgen]]}}.<ref name="JNTO">{{cite web |language=en |url=http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |title=Shinto Shrines |website=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=May 4, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070208060732/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |archive-date=February 8, 2007}}.</ref> A number of traditions of folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} exist:<ref name="EOS">{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1013 |language=en |title=''Performing Arts: Kagura''|author=Takayama Shigeru|date=February 20, 2007 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |agency=[[Kokugakuin University]]|access-date=June 7, 2019}}.</ref>
[[File:Urayasu-no-Mai-Kagura.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}}]] *{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}} – dances performed by shrine maidens ({{transliteration|ja|[[miko]]}}) originally derived from ritual dances in which the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} channeled the {{transliteration|ja|kami}}, as part of Imperial Court dances. These originally had a very loose form, akin to similar god-possession dances and rituals, but over time they have developed, into highly regular set forms. Today, they are performed by shrines during the {{nihongo||大嘗祭|daijō-sai}} festival and in worship to {{transliteration|ja|kami}} as part of a {{transliteration|ja|[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]}}. They are also performed at [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] as a [[:en:Martial arts|Martial arts]] performance. These dances are often performed with ritual props, such as bells, bamboo canes, sprigs of {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]]}}, or [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|paper streamers]]. *{{transliteration|ja|Izumo-ryū kagura}} – dances based on those performed at [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]] serve a number of purposes, including [[ritual purification]], celebration of auspicious days, and the reenactment of folktales. Originally quite popular in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]], near [[:en:Izumo|Izumo]], these dances have spread across the country, and have developed over the centuries, becoming more of a secular folk entertainment and less of a formal religious ritual. The sacred dance of the Sada shrine has been inscribed on the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] list of [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] since 2011.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-sada-shin-noh-danse-sacree-au-sanctuaire-de-sada-shimane-00412 |title=Le ''Sada shin noh'', danse sacrée au sanctuaire de Sada, Shimane|website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> * {{transliteration|ja|Hayachine Kagura}} – a form of dances derived from [[Yamabushi]] (a mobile group that embraces ascetic lives to gain power). This genre stresses on the power and energy. The performers always wear masks and use tools such as drum and sword to represent the magical power processed by Yamabushi.<ref name=":1" /> It was inscribed in 2009 as an [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-kagura-dhayachine-00272 |title=Le ''kagura'' d'Hayachine |website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> *{{transliteration|ja|Yutate kagura}} – a form of dances where {{transliteration|ja|miko}} and priests dip bamboo leaves in hot water and splash the hot water on themselves by shaking the leaves, and then scattering the hot water to people around the area. *{{transliteration|ja|Shishi kagura}} – a form of [[:en:Lion dance|Lion dance]], in which a group of dancers take on the role of the lion ({{transliteration|ja|shishi}}) and parade around the town. The lion mask and costume is seen as, in some ways, embodying the spirit of the lion, and this is a form of folk worship and ritual, as other forms of lion dances are in Japan and elsewhere. *{{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} – a form of dance deriving from rituals performed by traveling priests between [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], who would travel to villages, crossroads, and other locations to help the locals by driving away evil spirits. Acrobatic feats and lion dances played a major role in these rituals.
Around the time of the beginning of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] (1603–1868), performances derived from this emerged in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a major form of entertainment. In connection with the celebrations surrounding the beginning of the shogunate, lion dances, acrobatics, juggling, and a great variety of other entertainments were performed on stages across the city, all nominally under the auspices of {{transliteration|ja|daikagura}}. Over the course of the period, these came to be more closely associated with {{transliteration|ja|[[rakugo]]}} storytelling and other forms of popular entertainment. {{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} continues to be performed to this day and include many elements of street entertainment.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Type of ceremonial dance in Shinto ritual}} {{about|the Japanese dance}} {{Italic title}} [[File:The Kagura Dancer, by Suzuki Harunobu, circa 1766.jpg|thumb|''The Kagura Dancer'', by [[:en:Suzuki Harunobu|Suzuki Harunobu]]. [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{circa|1766}}]] {{Shinto}}
{{Nihongo3|"god-entertainment"|{{ruby|神楽|かぐら}}|'''Kagura'''|lead=yes}} is a type of [[Shinto]] ritual ceremonial dance. The term is a contraction of the phrase {{nihongo3|"seat of god"||kami no kura}}, indicating the presence of gods ({{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}) in the practice.
One major function of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} is {{nihongo3|purifying and shaking the spirit||chinkon}}, involving a procession-trance process. Usually a female shaman will perform the dance and obtain the oracle from the god—in the setting, the dancer herself turns into the god during the performance.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Averbuch|first=Irit|date=1998|title=Shamanic Dance in Japan: The Choreography of Possession in Kagura Performance|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/1178756|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=57|issue=2|pages=293–329|doi=10.2307/1178756|jstor=1178756|issn=0385-2342}}</ref> Once strictly a ceremonial art derived from {{Nihongo3|"oracular divinification"|神懸|kamigakari}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} has evolved in many directions over the span of more than a millennium. Today, it is very much a living tradition, with rituals tied to the rhythms of the agricultural calendar, thriving primarily in parts of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], and urban centers such as [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Petersen |first=David |year=2007 |title=Invitation to Kagura: Hidden Gem of the Traditional Japanese Performing Arts |location=Morrisville |publisher=Lulu Press}}</ref>{{sps|certain=y|reason=vanity publisher, trips edit filter|date=July 2024}}
==Types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Kagura at Festival in Japan (1914 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|thumb|Kagura dancing on a platform, 1914]] There are two major types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} and {{transliteration|ja|odori}}. {{transliteration|ja|Mai}} consists of slow circular movement, stressing quiet and elegance, while {{transliteration|ja|odori}} consists of quick leaping and jumping, stressing activation and energy. The two types can be understood as two phases of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} is a preparation process for trance and {{transliteration|ja|odori}} is the unconscious trance stage.
During {{transliteration|ja|mai}}, the female shaman, surrounded by a group of priests, holds a {{transliteration|ja|[[gohei]]}} (a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify) as well as sound-producing instruments and engages with circling movement to summon deities. Once the female shaman enters a possessed state, she switches into the spontaneous leaping movements of {{transliteration|ja|odori}}.<ref name=":0" />
==History== The epics {{transliteration|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} describe a folktale origin for the dances. In these texts, there is a famous legendary tale about the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who retreated into a cave, bringing darkness and cold to the world. [[Ame-no-Uzume]], goddess of the dawn and of revelry, led the other gods in a wild dance, and persuaded Amaterasu to emerge to see what the ruckus was all about.<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, part 1, page 44f. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Kagura}} is one of a number of rituals and arts said to derive from this event.
Originally called {{Nihongo||神座|kamukura/kamikura}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} began as sacred dances performed at the Imperial court by {{nihongo|shrine maidens||[[miko]]}} who were supposedly descendants of Ame-no-Uzume. During the performance, the shrine maidens usually utilize a channeling device for god such as masks and spears to imitate the trance. With the {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} music that has the power to summon the gods, the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} start to dance to transform themselves into the representation of the gods and receive messages as well as blessings from the deities.<ref name=":0" /> In 1871, Iwami Shinto offices claimed that theatrical {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performed by priests in the west of Japan demeaned their dignity and therefore banned the performance. Owing to the support of civilian performing groups at that time, the performance pieces were still preserved.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Lancashire|first=Terence|date=2001|title="Kagura" - A "Shinto" Dance? Or Perhaps Not|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/834231|journal=Asian Music|volume=33|issue=1|pages=25–59|doi=10.2307/834231|jstor=834231|issn=0044-9202}}</ref>
Over time, however, these {{Nihongo||御神楽|mikagura}} performed within the sacred and private precincts of the Imperial courts, inspired popular ritual dances, called {{Nihongo||里神楽|satokagura}}, which, being popular forms, practiced in villages all around the country, were adapted into various other folk traditions and developed into a number of different forms. Among these are {{transliteration|ja|miko kagura}}, {{transliteration|ja|shishi kagura}}, and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]]-style and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo]]-style {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} dances. Many more variations have developed over the centuries, including some which are fairly new, and most of which have become highly secularized folk traditions.
{{transliteration|ja|Kagura}}, in particular those forms that involve storytelling or reenactment of fables, is also one of the primary influences on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theatre.
<gallery> File:Kagura-Dance-Shinto-1914.png|Kagura dance in 1914 File:Shinto gohei.jpeg|{{transliteration|ja|Gohei}} – a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify File:Kagura villain.jpg|Mask from contemporary {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performance </gallery>
==Imperial {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Nikko_Toshogu_Yomeimon_Kagura_M4482.jpg|thumb|[[Kagura-den]] at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]]] {{Nihongo||御神楽|Mikagura}} is a ritual dance performed at the imperial court and at important [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]: [[:en:Kamo-jinja|Kamo-jinja]] and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]].<ref name="EOS"/> It consists of welcoming, entertaining and greeting the deities with humorous or poetic syllabic songs. Today it is sometimes considered as a sub-genre of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}}, of which it is one of the influences.<ref>{{Cite book| language=en | first1= John | last1=Whitney Hall | first2=Delmer | last2=M. Brown | first3=Marius | last3=B. Jansen |title= The Cambridge History of Japan |volume=1 |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=1993 |pages=650 | isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C | access-date=June 7, 2019 }}</ref> It predated Chinese inspiration, and has indigenous elements as well as influences from other elements such as {{Nihongo||管弦|kangen}}, {{Nihongo||舞楽|[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]}} and {{Nihongo||催馬楽|[[:en:Saibara|Saibara]]}}, which are forms of {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}},<ref name="Tanba">{{cite web |author= Akira Tanba |title= ''Musiques traditionnelles du Japon'' |publisher= Actes Sud |orig-date=First published in 1995. |url=https://www.actes-sud.fr/node/17787}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| author=S. Kishibe |title=''The Traditional Music of Japan, Ongaku no tomo edition'' |date=March 1984 |location=Tokyo |orig-date=1984 |page=42 |publisher=Ongaku No Tomo Sha Corp |url=https://www.amazon.com/Traditional-Music-Japan-Shigeo-Kishibe/dp/B0007B3OFK}}</ref> More simply, {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} can be considered dances accompanied by {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}} music.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://pad.philharmoniedeparis.fr/musiques-du-japon.aspx |title=Musiques du Japon |author=Véronique Brindeau |website=Philharmonie de Paris à la demande |access-date= June 29, 2020 }}.</ref>
The {{transliteration|ja|kagura-uta}} is the sacred vocal repertoire of 26 songs ({{transliteration|ja|Niwabi, Achime, Sakaki, Karakami, Hayakarakami, Komomakura, Sazanami, Senzai, Hayauta, Hoshi, Asakura, Sonokoma}}, etc.) traditionally performed by a male choir for several days, but reduced today to 12 chants performed in six hours.<ref name="Tanba"/> Instruments used include the {{Nihongo||笛|[[:en:Yokobue|Yokobue]]}} and/or the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]]}}, with the possible addition of a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Yamatogoto|Yamatogoto]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shakubyoshi|Shakubyoshi]]}} [[:en:Claves|Claves]].<ref name="EOS"/>
There are several {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} dances, including:<ref name="Tanba"/> * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Japan"|大和舞|Yamato Mai}}, associated with the {{Nihongo3|"song of Japan"|大和歌|Yamato uta}}, making use of the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ryūteki|Ryūteki]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and a pair of {{transliteration|ja|shakubyōshi}}, and with or without a [[:en:Zither|Zither]]; * The {{Nihongo3|"game of Oriental countries"|東遊|Azuma Asobi}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komabue|Komabue]]}}; * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Kume"|久米舞|Kume Mai}}, associated with the {{transliteration|ja|kume uta}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagurabue|Kagurabue]]}}, the {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and the {{transliteration|ja|wagon}}.
The formal Imperial ritual dances are performed in a number of sacred places and on a number of special occasions. At the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries|Imperial Sanctuary]], where the [[:en:Yata no kagami|Yata no kagami]] is kept, they are performed as part of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}} court music. {{transliteration|ja|Mikagura}} are also performed at the Imperial harvest festival and at major shrines such as [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]], [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo]], and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]]. Since around the year 1000, these events have taken place every year.
According to the [[:en:Board of Ceremonies|Board of Ceremonies]] of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} still take place every December in the Imperial Sanctuary and at the Imperial harvest festival ceremonies.
==Folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Susanoo-Orochi.jpg|thumb|[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[:en:Orochi|Orochi]] in Izumo-ryū {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}]] {{transliteration|ja|Satokagura}}, or "normal {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}", is a wide umbrella term containing a great diversity of folk dances derived from the {{nihongo|Imperial ritual dances||mikagura}}, and incorporated with other folk traditions. It is the partial origin of both [[:en:Noh|Noh]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[kyōgen]]}}.<ref name="JNTO">{{cite web |language=en |url=http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |title=Shinto Shrines |website=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=May 4, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070208060732/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |archive-date=February 8, 2007}}.</ref> A number of traditions of folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} exist:<ref name="EOS">{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1013 |language=en |title=''Performing Arts: Kagura''|author=Takayama Shigeru|date=February 20, 2007 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |agency=[[Kokugakuin University]]|access-date=June 7, 2019}}.</ref>
[[File:Urayasu-no-Mai-Kagura.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}}]] *{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}} – dances performed by shrine maidens ({{transliteration|ja|[[miko]]}}) originally derived from ritual dances in which the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} channeled the {{transliteration|ja|kami}}, as part of Imperial Court dances. These originally had a very loose form, akin to similar god-possession dances and rituals, but over time they have developed, into highly regular set forms. Today, they are performed by shrines during the {{nihongo||大嘗祭|daijō-sai}} festival and in worship to {{transliteration|ja|kami}} as part of a {{transliteration|ja|[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]}}. They are also performed at [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] as a [[:en:Martial arts|Martial arts]] performance. These dances are often performed with ritual props, such as bells, bamboo canes, sprigs of {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]]}}, or [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|paper streamers]]. *{{transliteration|ja|Izumo-ryū kagura}} – dances based on those performed at [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]] serve a number of purposes, including [[ritual purification]], celebration of auspicious days, and the reenactment of folktales. Originally quite popular in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]], near [[:en:Izumo|Izumo]], these dances have spread across the country, and have developed over the centuries, becoming more of a secular folk entertainment and less of a formal religious ritual. The sacred dance of the Sada shrine has been inscribed on the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] list of [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] since 2011.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-sada-shin-noh-danse-sacree-au-sanctuaire-de-sada-shimane-00412 |title=Le ''Sada shin noh'', danse sacrée au sanctuaire de Sada, Shimane|website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> * {{transliteration|ja|Hayachine Kagura}} – a form of dances derived from [[Yamabushi]] (a mobile group that embraces ascetic lives to gain power). This genre stresses on the power and energy. The performers always wear masks and use tools such as drum and sword to represent the magical power processed by Yamabushi.<ref name=":1" /> It was inscribed in 2009 as an [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-kagura-dhayachine-00272 |title=Le ''kagura'' d'Hayachine |website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> *{{transliteration|ja|Yutate kagura}} – a form of dances where {{transliteration|ja|miko}} and priests dip bamboo leaves in hot water and splash the hot water on themselves by shaking the leaves, and then scattering the hot water to people around the area. *{{transliteration|ja|Shishi kagura}} – a form of [[:en:Lion dance|Lion dance]], in which a group of dancers take on the role of the lion ({{transliteration|ja|shishi}}) and parade around the town. The lion mask and costume is seen as, in some ways, embodying the spirit of the lion, and this is a form of folk worship and ritual, as other forms of lion dances are in Japan and elsewhere. *{{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} – a form of dance deriving from rituals performed by traveling priests between [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], who would travel to villages, crossroads, and other locations to help the locals by driving away evil spirits. Acrobatic feats and lion dances played a major role in these rituals.
Around the time of the beginning of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] (1603–1868), performances derived from this emerged in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a major form of entertainment. In connection with the celebrations surrounding the beginning of the shogunate, lion dances, acrobatics, juggling, and a great variety of other entertainments were performed on stages across the city, all nominally under the auspices of {{transliteration|ja|daikagura}}. Over the course of the period, these came to be more closely associated with {{transliteration|ja|[[rakugo]]}} storytelling and other forms of popular entertainment. {{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} continues to be performed to this day and include many elements of street entertainment.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{transliteration|ja|[[Gagaku]]}} * [[Mystery play]]s * {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}}
==References== {{reflist}} *Derived primarily from the Japanese Wikipedia article. *Giolai Andrea, ''Introducing Mikagura.Some Ethnomusicological Features of an Ancient Japanese Ritual'', https://web.archive.org/web/20160316132415/http://www.centrostudiorientaliroma.net/introducing-mikagura-%e5%be%a1%e7%a5%9e%e6%a5%bd-some-ethnomusicological-features-of-an-ancient-japanese-ritual/?lang=en

==Imperial {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Nikko_Toshogu_Yomeimon_Kagura_M4482.jpg|thumb|[[Kagura-den]] at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]]] {{Nihongo||御神楽|Mikagura}} is a ritual dance performed at the imperial court and at important [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]: [[:en:Kamo-jinja|Kamo-jinja]] and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]].<ref name="EOS"/> It consists of welcoming, entertaining and greeting the deities with humorous or poetic syllabic songs. Today it is sometimes considered as a sub-genre of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}}, of which it is one of the influences.<ref>{{Cite book| language=en | first1= John | last1=Whitney Hall | first2=Delmer | last2=M. Brown | first3=Marius | last3=B. Jansen |title= The Cambridge History of Japan |volume=1 |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=1993 |pages=650 | isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C | access-date=June 7, 2019 }}</ref> It predated Chinese inspiration, and has indigenous elements as well as influences from other elements such as {{Nihongo||管弦|kangen}}, {{Nihongo||舞楽|[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]}} and {{Nihongo||催馬楽|[[:en:Saibara|Saibara]]}}, which are forms of {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}},<ref name="Tanba">{{cite web |author= Akira Tanba |title= ''Musiques traditionnelles du Japon'' |publisher= Actes Sud |orig-date=First published in 1995. |url=https://www.actes-sud.fr/node/17787}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| author=S. Kishibe |title=''The Traditional Music of Japan, Ongaku no tomo edition'' |date=March 1984 |location=Tokyo |orig-date=1984 |page=42 |publisher=Ongaku No Tomo Sha Corp |url=https://www.amazon.com/Traditional-Music-Japan-Shigeo-Kishibe/dp/B0007B3OFK}}</ref> More simply, {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} can be considered dances accompanied by {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}} music.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://pad.philharmoniedeparis.fr/musiques-du-japon.aspx |title=Musiques du Japon |author=Véronique Brindeau |website=Philharmonie de Paris à la demande |access-date= June 29, 2020 }}.</ref>
The {{transliteration|ja|kagura-uta}} is the sacred vocal repertoire of 26 songs ({{transliteration|ja|Niwabi, Achime, Sakaki, Karakami, Hayakarakami, Komomakura, Sazanami, Senzai, Hayauta, Hoshi, Asakura, Sonokoma}}, etc.) traditionally performed by a male choir for several days, but reduced today to 12 chants performed in six hours.<ref name="Tanba"/> Instruments used include the {{Nihongo||笛|[[:en:Yokobue|Yokobue]]}} and/or the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]]}}, with the possible addition of a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Yamatogoto|Yamatogoto]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shakubyoshi|Shakubyoshi]]}} [[:en:Claves|Claves]].<ref name="EOS"/>
There are several {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} dances, including:<ref name="Tanba"/> * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Japan"|大和舞|Yamato Mai}}, associated with the {{Nihongo3|"song of Japan"|大和歌|Yamato uta}}, making use of the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ryūteki|Ryūteki]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and a pair of {{transliteration|ja|shakubyōshi}}, and with or without a [[:en:Zither|Zither]]; * The {{Nihongo3|"game of Oriental countries"|東遊|Azuma Asobi}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komabue|Komabue]]}}; * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Kume"|久米舞|Kume Mai}}, associated with the {{transliteration|ja|kume uta}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagurabue|Kagurabue]]}}, the {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and the {{transliteration|ja|wagon}}.
The formal Imperial ritual dances are performed in a number of sacred places and on a number of special occasions. At the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries|Imperial Sanctuary]], where the [[:en:Yata no kagami|Yata no kagami]] is kept, they are performed as part of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}} court music. {{transliteration|ja|Mikagura}} are also performed at the Imperial harvest festival and at major shrines such as [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]], [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo]], and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]]. Since around the year 1000, these events have taken place every year.
According to the [[:en:Board of Ceremonies|Board of Ceremonies]] of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} still take place every December in the Imperial Sanctuary and at the Imperial harvest festival ceremonies.
==Folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Susanoo-Orochi.jpg|thumb|[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[:en:Orochi|Orochi]] in Izumo-ryū {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}]] {{transliteration|ja|Satokagura}}, or "normal {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}", is a wide umbrella term containing a great diversity of folk dances derived from the {{nihongo|Imperial ritual dances||mikagura}}, and incorporated with other folk traditions. It is the partial origin of both [[:en:Noh|Noh]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[kyōgen]]}}.<ref name="JNTO">{{cite web |language=en |url=http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |title=Shinto Shrines |website=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=May 4, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070208060732/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |archive-date=February 8, 2007}}.</ref> A number of traditions of folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} exist:<ref name="EOS">{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1013 |language=en |title=''Performing Arts: Kagura''|author=Takayama Shigeru|date=February 20, 2007 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |agency=[[Kokugakuin University]]|access-date=June 7, 2019}}.</ref>
[[File:Urayasu-no-Mai-Kagura.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}}]] *{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}} – dances performed by shrine maidens ({{transliteration|ja|[[miko]]}}) originally derived from ritual dances in which the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} channeled the {{transliteration|ja|kami}}, as part of Imperial Court dances. These originally had a very loose form, akin to similar god-possession dances and rituals, but over time they have developed, into highly regular set forms. Today, they are performed by shrines during the {{nihongo||大嘗祭|daijō-sai}} festival and in worship to {{transliteration|ja|kami}} as part of a {{transliteration|ja|[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]}}. They are also performed at [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] as a [[:en:Martial arts|Martial arts]] performance. These dances are often performed with ritual props, such as bells, bamboo canes, sprigs of {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]]}}, or [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|paper streamers]]. *{{transliteration|ja|Izumo-ryū kagura}} – dances based on those performed at [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]] serve a number of purposes, including [[ritual purification]], celebration of auspicious days, and the reenactment of folktales. Originally quite popular in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]], near [[:en:Izumo|Izumo]], these dances have spread across the country, and have developed over the centuries, becoming more of a secular folk entertainment and less of a formal religious ritual. The sacred dance of the Sada shrine has been inscribed on the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] list of [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] since 2011.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-sada-shin-noh-danse-sacree-au-sanctuaire-de-sada-shimane-00412 |title=Le ''Sada shin noh'', danse sacrée au sanctuaire de Sada, Shimane|website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> * {{transliteration|ja|Hayachine Kagura}} – a form of dances derived from [[Yamabushi]] (a mobile group that embraces ascetic lives to gain power). This genre stresses on the power and energy. The performers always wear masks and use tools such as drum and sword to represent the magical power processed by Yamabushi.<ref name=":1" /> It was inscribed in 2009 as an [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-kagura-dhayachine-00272 |title=Le ''kagura'' d'Hayachine |website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> *{{transliteration|ja|Yutate kagura}} – a form of dances where {{transliteration|ja|miko}} and priests dip bamboo leaves in hot water and splash the hot water on themselves by shaking the leaves, and then scattering the hot water to people around the area. *{{transliteration|ja|Shishi kagura}} – a form of [[:en:Lion dance|Lion dance]], in which a group of dancers take on the role of the lion ({{transliteration|ja|shishi}}) and parade around the town. The lion mask and costume is seen as, in some ways, embodying the spirit of the lion, and this is a form of folk worship and ritual, as other forms of lion dances are in Japan and elsewhere. *{{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} – a form of dance deriving from rituals performed by traveling priests between [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], who would travel to villages, crossroads, and other locations to help the locals by driving away evil spirits. Acrobatic feats and lion dances played a major role in these rituals.
Around the time of the beginning of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] (1603–1868), performances derived from this emerged in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a major form of entertainment. In connection with the celebrations surrounding the beginning of the shogunate, lion dances, acrobatics, juggling, and a great variety of other entertainments were performed on stages across the city, all nominally under the auspices of {{transliteration|ja|daikagura}}. Over the course of the period, these came to be more closely associated with {{transliteration|ja|[[rakugo]]}} storytelling and other forms of popular entertainment. {{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} continues to be performed to this day and include many elements of street entertainment.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاغورا]][[ca:Kagura]][[cs:Kagura]][[de:Kagura]][[en:Kagura]][[es:Kagura (danza)]][[fa:کاگورا]][[fi:Kagura]][[fr:Kagura]][[id:Kagura]][[it:Kagura (danza)]][[ja:神楽]][[ko:가구라]][[lt:Kagura]][[or:କାଗୁରା]][[pl:Kagura]][[pt:Kagura]][[ru:Кагура]][[sq:Kagura]][[te:కగుర (నృత్య రీతి)]][[tr:Kagura]][[uk:Каґура]][[vi:Kagura]][[zh:神樂]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Type of ceremonial dance in Shinto ritual}} {{about|the Japanese dance}} {{Italic title}} [[File:The Kagura Dancer, by Suzuki Harunobu, circa 1766.jpg|thumb|''The Kagura Dancer'', by [[:en:Suzuki Harunobu|Suzuki Harunobu]]. [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{circa|1766}}]] {{Shinto}}
{{Nihongo3|"god-entertainment"|{{ruby|神楽|かぐら}}|'''Kagura'''|lead=yes}} is a type of [[Shinto]] ritual ceremonial dance. The term is a contraction of the phrase {{nihongo3|"seat of god"||kami no kura}}, indicating the presence of gods ({{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}) in the practice.
One major function of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} is {{nihongo3|purifying and shaking the spirit||chinkon}}, involving a procession-trance process. Usually a female shaman will perform the dance and obtain the oracle from the god—in the setting, the dancer herself turns into the god during the performance.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Averbuch|first=Irit|date=1998|title=Shamanic Dance in Japan: The Choreography of Possession in Kagura Performance|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/1178756|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=57|issue=2|pages=293–329|doi=10.2307/1178756|jstor=1178756|issn=0385-2342}}</ref> Once strictly a ceremonial art derived from {{Nihongo3|"oracular divinification"|神懸|kamigakari}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} has evolved in many directions over the span of more than a millennium. Today, it is very much a living tradition, with rituals tied to the rhythms of the agricultural calendar, thriving primarily in parts of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], and urban centers such as [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Petersen |first=David |year=2007 |title=Invitation to Kagura: Hidden Gem of the Traditional Japanese Performing Arts |location=Morrisville |publisher=Lulu Press}}</ref>{{sps|certain=y|reason=vanity publisher, trips edit filter|date=July 2024}}
==Types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Kagura at Festival in Japan (1914 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|thumb|Kagura dancing on a platform, 1914]] There are two major types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} and {{transliteration|ja|odori}}. {{transliteration|ja|Mai}} consists of slow circular movement, stressing quiet and elegance, while {{transliteration|ja|odori}} consists of quick leaping and jumping, stressing activation and energy. The two types can be understood as two phases of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} is a preparation process for trance and {{transliteration|ja|odori}} is the unconscious trance stage.
During {{transliteration|ja|mai}}, the female shaman, surrounded by a group of priests, holds a {{transliteration|ja|[[gohei]]}} (a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify) as well as sound-producing instruments and engages with circling movement to summon deities. Once the female shaman enters a possessed state, she switches into the spontaneous leaping movements of {{transliteration|ja|odori}}.<ref name=":0" />
==History== The epics {{transliteration|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} describe a folktale origin for the dances. In these texts, there is a famous legendary tale about the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who retreated into a cave, bringing darkness and cold to the world. [[Ame-no-Uzume]], goddess of the dawn and of revelry, led the other gods in a wild dance, and persuaded Amaterasu to emerge to see what the ruckus was all about.<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, part 1, page 44f. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Kagura}} is one of a number of rituals and arts said to derive from this event.
Originally called {{Nihongo||神座|kamukura/kamikura}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} began as sacred dances performed at the Imperial court by {{nihongo|shrine maidens||[[miko]]}} who were supposedly descendants of Ame-no-Uzume. During the performance, the shrine maidens usually utilize a channeling device for god such as masks and spears to imitate the trance. With the {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} music that has the power to summon the gods, the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} start to dance to transform themselves into the representation of the gods and receive messages as well as blessings from the deities.<ref name=":0" /> In 1871, Iwami Shinto offices claimed that theatrical {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performed by priests in the west of Japan demeaned their dignity and therefore banned the performance. Owing to the support of civilian performing groups at that time, the performance pieces were still preserved.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Lancashire|first=Terence|date=2001|title="Kagura" - A "Shinto" Dance? Or Perhaps Not|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/834231|journal=Asian Music|volume=33|issue=1|pages=25–59|doi=10.2307/834231|jstor=834231|issn=0044-9202}}</ref>
Over time, however, these {{Nihongo||御神楽|mikagura}} performed within the sacred and private precincts of the Imperial courts, inspired popular ritual dances, called {{Nihongo||里神楽|satokagura}}, which, being popular forms, practiced in villages all around the country, were adapted into various other folk traditions and developed into a number of different forms. Among these are {{transliteration|ja|miko kagura}}, {{transliteration|ja|shishi kagura}}, and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]]-style and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo]]-style {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} dances. Many more variations have developed over the centuries, including some which are fairly new, and most of which have become highly secularized folk traditions.
{{transliteration|ja|Kagura}}, in particular those forms that involve storytelling or reenactment of fables, is also one of the primary influences on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theatre.
<gallery> File:Kagura-Dance-Shinto-1914.png|Kagura dance in 1914 File:Shinto gohei.jpeg|{{transliteration|ja|Gohei}} – a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify File:Kagura villain.jpg|Mask from contemporary {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performance </gallery>
==Imperial {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Nikko_Toshogu_Yomeimon_Kagura_M4482.jpg|thumb|[[Kagura-den]] at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]]] {{Nihongo||御神楽|Mikagura}} is a ritual dance performed at the imperial court and at important [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]: [[:en:Kamo-jinja|Kamo-jinja]] and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]].<ref name="EOS"/> It consists of welcoming, entertaining and greeting the deities with humorous or poetic syllabic songs. Today it is sometimes considered as a sub-genre of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}}, of which it is one of the influences.<ref>{{Cite book| language=en | first1= John | last1=Whitney Hall | first2=Delmer | last2=M. Brown | first3=Marius | last3=B. Jansen |title= The Cambridge History of Japan |volume=1 |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=1993 |pages=650 | isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C | access-date=June 7, 2019 }}</ref> It predated Chinese inspiration, and has indigenous elements as well as influences from other elements such as {{Nihongo||管弦|kangen}}, {{Nihongo||舞楽|[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]}} and {{Nihongo||催馬楽|[[:en:Saibara|Saibara]]}}, which are forms of {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}},<ref name="Tanba">{{cite web |author= Akira Tanba |title= ''Musiques traditionnelles du Japon'' |publisher= Actes Sud |orig-date=First published in 1995. |url=https://www.actes-sud.fr/node/17787}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| author=S. Kishibe |title=''The Traditional Music of Japan, Ongaku no tomo edition'' |date=March 1984 |location=Tokyo |orig-date=1984 |page=42 |publisher=Ongaku No Tomo Sha Corp |url=https://www.amazon.com/Traditional-Music-Japan-Shigeo-Kishibe/dp/B0007B3OFK}}</ref> More simply, {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} can be considered dances accompanied by {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}} music.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://pad.philharmoniedeparis.fr/musiques-du-japon.aspx |title=Musiques du Japon |author=Véronique Brindeau |website=Philharmonie de Paris à la demande |access-date= June 29, 2020 }}.</ref>
The {{transliteration|ja|kagura-uta}} is the sacred vocal repertoire of 26 songs ({{transliteration|ja|Niwabi, Achime, Sakaki, Karakami, Hayakarakami, Komomakura, Sazanami, Senzai, Hayauta, Hoshi, Asakura, Sonokoma}}, etc.) traditionally performed by a male choir for several days, but reduced today to 12 chants performed in six hours.<ref name="Tanba"/> Instruments used include the {{Nihongo||笛|[[:en:Yokobue|Yokobue]]}} and/or the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]]}}, with the possible addition of a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Yamatogoto|Yamatogoto]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shakubyoshi|Shakubyoshi]]}} [[:en:Claves|Claves]].<ref name="EOS"/>
There are several {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} dances, including:<ref name="Tanba"/> * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Japan"|大和舞|Yamato Mai}}, associated with the {{Nihongo3|"song of Japan"|大和歌|Yamato uta}}, making use of the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ryūteki|Ryūteki]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and a pair of {{transliteration|ja|shakubyōshi}}, and with or without a [[:en:Zither|Zither]]; * The {{Nihongo3|"game of Oriental countries"|東遊|Azuma Asobi}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komabue|Komabue]]}}; * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Kume"|久米舞|Kume Mai}}, associated with the {{transliteration|ja|kume uta}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagurabue|Kagurabue]]}}, the {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and the {{transliteration|ja|wagon}}.
The formal Imperial ritual dances are performed in a number of sacred places and on a number of special occasions. At the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries|Imperial Sanctuary]], where the [[:en:Yata no kagami|Yata no kagami]] is kept, they are performed as part of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}} court music. {{transliteration|ja|Mikagura}} are also performed at the Imperial harvest festival and at major shrines such as [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]], [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo]], and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]]. Since around the year 1000, these events have taken place every year.
According to the [[:en:Board of Ceremonies|Board of Ceremonies]] of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} still take place every December in the Imperial Sanctuary and at the Imperial harvest festival ceremonies.
==Folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Susanoo-Orochi.jpg|thumb|[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[:en:Orochi|Orochi]] in Izumo-ryū {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}]] {{transliteration|ja|Satokagura}}, or "normal {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}", is a wide umbrella term containing a great diversity of folk dances derived from the {{nihongo|Imperial ritual dances||mikagura}}, and incorporated with other folk traditions. It is the partial origin of both [[:en:Noh|Noh]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[kyōgen]]}}.<ref name="JNTO">{{cite web |language=en |url=http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |title=Shinto Shrines |website=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=May 4, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070208060732/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |archive-date=February 8, 2007}}.</ref> A number of traditions of folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} exist:<ref name="EOS">{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1013 |language=en |title=''Performing Arts: Kagura''|author=Takayama Shigeru|date=February 20, 2007 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |agency=[[Kokugakuin University]]|access-date=June 7, 2019}}.</ref>
[[File:Urayasu-no-Mai-Kagura.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}}]] *{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}} – dances performed by shrine maidens ({{transliteration|ja|[[miko]]}}) originally derived from ritual dances in which the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} channeled the {{transliteration|ja|kami}}, as part of Imperial Court dances. These originally had a very loose form, akin to similar god-possession dances and rituals, but over time they have developed, into highly regular set forms. Today, they are performed by shrines during the {{nihongo||大嘗祭|daijō-sai}} festival and in worship to {{transliteration|ja|kami}} as part of a {{transliteration|ja|[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]}}. They are also performed at [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] as a [[:en:Martial arts|Martial arts]] performance. These dances are often performed with ritual props, such as bells, bamboo canes, sprigs of {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]]}}, or [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|paper streamers]]. *{{transliteration|ja|Izumo-ryū kagura}} – dances based on those performed at [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]] serve a number of purposes, including [[ritual purification]], celebration of auspicious days, and the reenactment of folktales. Originally quite popular in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]], near [[:en:Izumo|Izumo]], these dances have spread across the country, and have developed over the centuries, becoming more of a secular folk entertainment and less of a formal religious ritual. The sacred dance of the Sada shrine has been inscribed on the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] list of [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] since 2011.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-sada-shin-noh-danse-sacree-au-sanctuaire-de-sada-shimane-00412 |title=Le ''Sada shin noh'', danse sacrée au sanctuaire de Sada, Shimane|website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> * {{transliteration|ja|Hayachine Kagura}} – a form of dances derived from [[Yamabushi]] (a mobile group that embraces ascetic lives to gain power). This genre stresses on the power and energy. The performers always wear masks and use tools such as drum and sword to represent the magical power processed by Yamabushi.<ref name=":1" /> It was inscribed in 2009 as an [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-kagura-dhayachine-00272 |title=Le ''kagura'' d'Hayachine |website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> *{{transliteration|ja|Yutate kagura}} – a form of dances where {{transliteration|ja|miko}} and priests dip bamboo leaves in hot water and splash the hot water on themselves by shaking the leaves, and then scattering the hot water to people around the area. *{{transliteration|ja|Shishi kagura}} – a form of [[:en:Lion dance|Lion dance]], in which a group of dancers take on the role of the lion ({{transliteration|ja|shishi}}) and parade around the town. The lion mask and costume is seen as, in some ways, embodying the spirit of the lion, and this is a form of folk worship and ritual, as other forms of lion dances are in Japan and elsewhere. *{{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} – a form of dance deriving from rituals performed by traveling priests between [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], who would travel to villages, crossroads, and other locations to help the locals by driving away evil spirits. Acrobatic feats and lion dances played a major role in these rituals.
Around the time of the beginning of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] (1603–1868), performances derived from this emerged in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a major form of entertainment. In connection with the celebrations surrounding the beginning of the shogunate, lion dances, acrobatics, juggling, and a great variety of other entertainments were performed on stages across the city, all nominally under the auspices of {{transliteration|ja|daikagura}}. Over the course of the period, these came to be more closely associated with {{transliteration|ja|[[rakugo]]}} storytelling and other forms of popular entertainment. {{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} continues to be performed to this day and include many elements of street entertainment.
==See also== * [[Glossary of Shinto]] * {{transliteration|ja|[[Gagaku]]}} * [[Mystery play]]s * {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagura suzu|Kagura suzu]]}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:كاغورا]][[ca:Kagura]][[cs:Kagura]][[de:Kagura]][[en:Kagura]][[es:Kagura (danza)]][[fa:کاگورا]][[fi:Kagura]][[fr:Kagura]][[id:Kagura]][[it:Kagura (danza)]][[ja:神楽]][[ko:가구라]][[lt:Kagura]][[or:କାଗୁରା]][[pl:Kagura]][[pt:Kagura]][[ru:Кагура]][[sq:Kagura]][[te:కగుర (నృత్య రీతి)]][[tr:Kagura]][[uk:Каґура]][[vi:Kagura]][[zh:神樂]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1348260]] Wikidata name: Kagura [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Type of ceremonial dance in Shinto ritual}} {{about|the Japanese dance}} {{Italic title}} [[File:The Kagura Dancer, by Suzuki Harunobu, circa 1766.jpg|thumb|''The Kagura Dancer'', by [[:en:Suzuki Harunobu|Suzuki Harunobu]]. [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], {{circa|1766}}]] {{Shinto}}
{{Nihongo3|"god-entertainment"|{{ruby|神楽|かぐら}}|'''Kagura'''|lead=yes}} is a type of [[Shinto]] ritual ceremonial dance. The term is a contraction of the phrase {{nihongo3|"seat of god"||kami no kura}}, indicating the presence of gods ({{transliteration|ja|[[kami]]}}) in the practice.
One major function of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} is {{nihongo3|purifying and shaking the spirit||chinkon}}, involving a procession-trance process. Usually a female shaman will perform the dance and obtain the oracle from the god—in the setting, the dancer herself turns into the god during the performance.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last=Averbuch|first=Irit|date=1998|title=Shamanic Dance in Japan: The Choreography of Possession in Kagura Performance|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/1178756|journal=Asian Folklore Studies|volume=57|issue=2|pages=293–329|doi=10.2307/1178756|jstor=1178756|issn=0385-2342}}</ref> Once strictly a ceremonial art derived from {{Nihongo3|"oracular divinification"|神懸|kamigakari}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} has evolved in many directions over the span of more than a millennium. Today, it is very much a living tradition, with rituals tied to the rhythms of the agricultural calendar, thriving primarily in parts of [[:en:Shimane Prefecture|Shimane Prefecture]], and urban centers such as [[:en:Hiroshima|Hiroshima]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Petersen |first=David |year=2007 |title=Invitation to Kagura: Hidden Gem of the Traditional Japanese Performing Arts |location=Morrisville |publisher=Lulu Press}}</ref>{{sps|certain=y|reason=vanity publisher, trips edit filter|date=July 2024}}
==Types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Kagura at Festival in Japan (1914 by Elstner Hilton).jpg|thumb|Kagura dancing on a platform, 1914]] There are two major types of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} and {{transliteration|ja|odori}}. {{transliteration|ja|Mai}} consists of slow circular movement, stressing quiet and elegance, while {{transliteration|ja|odori}} consists of quick leaping and jumping, stressing activation and energy. The two types can be understood as two phases of {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}: {{transliteration|ja|mai}} is a preparation process for trance and {{transliteration|ja|odori}} is the unconscious trance stage.
During {{transliteration|ja|mai}}, the female shaman, surrounded by a group of priests, holds a {{transliteration|ja|[[gohei]]}} (a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify) as well as sound-producing instruments and engages with circling movement to summon deities. Once the female shaman enters a possessed state, she switches into the spontaneous leaping movements of {{transliteration|ja|odori}}.<ref name=":0" />
==History== The epics {{transliteration|ja|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} describe a folktale origin for the dances. In these texts, there is a famous legendary tale about the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], who retreated into a cave, bringing darkness and cold to the world. [[Ame-no-Uzume]], goddess of the dawn and of revelry, led the other gods in a wild dance, and persuaded Amaterasu to emerge to see what the ruckus was all about.<ref>''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, part 1, page 44f. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}</ref> {{transliteration|ja|Kagura}} is one of a number of rituals and arts said to derive from this event.
Originally called {{Nihongo||神座|kamukura/kamikura}}, {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} began as sacred dances performed at the Imperial court by {{nihongo|shrine maidens||[[miko]]}} who were supposedly descendants of Ame-no-Uzume. During the performance, the shrine maidens usually utilize a channeling device for god such as masks and spears to imitate the trance. With the {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} music that has the power to summon the gods, the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} start to dance to transform themselves into the representation of the gods and receive messages as well as blessings from the deities.<ref name=":0" /> In 1871, Iwami Shinto offices claimed that theatrical {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performed by priests in the west of Japan demeaned their dignity and therefore banned the performance. Owing to the support of civilian performing groups at that time, the performance pieces were still preserved.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Lancashire|first=Terence|date=2001|title="Kagura" - A "Shinto" Dance? Or Perhaps Not|url=http://dx.doi.org/10.2307/834231|journal=Asian Music|volume=33|issue=1|pages=25–59|doi=10.2307/834231|jstor=834231|issn=0044-9202}}</ref>
Over time, however, these {{Nihongo||御神楽|mikagura}} performed within the sacred and private precincts of the Imperial courts, inspired popular ritual dances, called {{Nihongo||里神楽|satokagura}}, which, being popular forms, practiced in villages all around the country, were adapted into various other folk traditions and developed into a number of different forms. Among these are {{transliteration|ja|miko kagura}}, {{transliteration|ja|shishi kagura}}, and [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]]-style and [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo]]-style {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} dances. Many more variations have developed over the centuries, including some which are fairly new, and most of which have become highly secularized folk traditions.
{{transliteration|ja|Kagura}}, in particular those forms that involve storytelling or reenactment of fables, is also one of the primary influences on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] theatre.
<gallery> File:Kagura-Dance-Shinto-1914.png|Kagura dance in 1914 File:Shinto gohei.jpeg|{{transliteration|ja|Gohei}} – a ceremonial wand used to cleanse or purify File:Kagura villain.jpg|Mask from contemporary {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} performance </gallery>
==Imperial {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Nikko_Toshogu_Yomeimon_Kagura_M4482.jpg|thumb|[[Kagura-den]] at [[:en:Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikkō Tōshō-gū]]]] {{Nihongo||御神楽|Mikagura}} is a ritual dance performed at the imperial court and at important [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]]: [[:en:Kamo-jinja|Kamo-jinja]] and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]].<ref name="EOS"/> It consists of welcoming, entertaining and greeting the deities with humorous or poetic syllabic songs. Today it is sometimes considered as a sub-genre of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}}, of which it is one of the influences.<ref>{{Cite book| language=en | first1= John | last1=Whitney Hall | first2=Delmer | last2=M. Brown | first3=Marius | last3=B. Jansen |title= The Cambridge History of Japan |volume=1 |publisher=[[Cambridge University Press]] |year=1993 |pages=650 | isbn=978-0-521-22352-2 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=A3_6lp8IOK8C | access-date=June 7, 2019 }}</ref> It predated Chinese inspiration, and has indigenous elements as well as influences from other elements such as {{Nihongo||管弦|kangen}}, {{Nihongo||舞楽|[[:en:Bugaku|Bugaku]]}} and {{Nihongo||催馬楽|[[:en:Saibara|Saibara]]}}, which are forms of {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}},<ref name="Tanba">{{cite web |author= Akira Tanba |title= ''Musiques traditionnelles du Japon'' |publisher= Actes Sud |orig-date=First published in 1995. |url=https://www.actes-sud.fr/node/17787}}</ref><ref>{{cite book| author=S. Kishibe |title=''The Traditional Music of Japan, Ongaku no tomo edition'' |date=March 1984 |location=Tokyo |orig-date=1984 |page=42 |publisher=Ongaku No Tomo Sha Corp |url=https://www.amazon.com/Traditional-Music-Japan-Shigeo-Kishibe/dp/B0007B3OFK}}</ref> More simply, {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} can be considered dances accompanied by {{transliteration|ja|gagaku}} music.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://pad.philharmoniedeparis.fr/musiques-du-japon.aspx |title=Musiques du Japon |author=Véronique Brindeau |website=Philharmonie de Paris à la demande |access-date= June 29, 2020 }}.</ref>
The {{transliteration|ja|kagura-uta}} is the sacred vocal repertoire of 26 songs ({{transliteration|ja|Niwabi, Achime, Sakaki, Karakami, Hayakarakami, Komomakura, Sazanami, Senzai, Hayauta, Hoshi, Asakura, Sonokoma}}, etc.) traditionally performed by a male choir for several days, but reduced today to 12 chants performed in six hours.<ref name="Tanba"/> Instruments used include the {{Nihongo||笛|[[:en:Yokobue|Yokobue]]}} and/or the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Hichiriki|Hichiriki]]}}, with the possible addition of a {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Yamatogoto|Yamatogoto]]}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Shakubyoshi|Shakubyoshi]]}} [[:en:Claves|Claves]].<ref name="EOS"/>
There are several {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} dances, including:<ref name="Tanba"/> * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Japan"|大和舞|Yamato Mai}}, associated with the {{Nihongo3|"song of Japan"|大和歌|Yamato uta}}, making use of the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Ryūteki|Ryūteki]]}} or {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and a pair of {{transliteration|ja|shakubyōshi}}, and with or without a [[:en:Zither|Zither]]; * The {{Nihongo3|"game of Oriental countries"|東遊|Azuma Asobi}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Komabue|Komabue]]}}; * The {{Nihongo3|"dance of Kume"|久米舞|Kume Mai}}, associated with the {{transliteration|ja|kume uta}}, using the {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Kagurabue|Kagurabue]]}}, the {{transliteration|ja|hichiriki}} and the {{transliteration|ja|wagon}}.
The formal Imperial ritual dances are performed in a number of sacred places and on a number of special occasions. At the [[Three Palace Sanctuaries|Imperial Sanctuary]], where the [[:en:Yata no kagami|Yata no kagami]] is kept, they are performed as part of {{transliteration|ja|[[gagaku]]}} court music. {{transliteration|ja|Mikagura}} are also performed at the Imperial harvest festival and at major shrines such as [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise]], [[:en:Kamo Shrines|Kamo]], and [[Iwashimizu Hachimangū|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū]]. Since around the year 1000, these events have taken place every year.
According to the [[:en:Board of Ceremonies|Board of Ceremonies]] of the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]], {{transliteration|ja|mikagura}} still take place every December in the Imperial Sanctuary and at the Imperial harvest festival ceremonies.
==Folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}== [[File:Susanoo-Orochi.jpg|thumb|[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[:en:Orochi|Orochi]] in Izumo-ryū {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}]] {{transliteration|ja|Satokagura}}, or "normal {{transliteration|ja|kagura}}", is a wide umbrella term containing a great diversity of folk dances derived from the {{nihongo|Imperial ritual dances||mikagura}}, and incorporated with other folk traditions. It is the partial origin of both [[:en:Noh|Noh]] and {{transliteration|ja|[[kyōgen]]}}.<ref name="JNTO">{{cite web |language=en |url=http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |title=Shinto Shrines |website=Japan National Tourism Organization |access-date=May 4, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070208060732/http://www.jnto.go.jp/eng/indepth/history/experience/d.html |archive-date=February 8, 2007}}.</ref> A number of traditions of folk {{transliteration|ja|kagura}} exist:<ref name="EOS">{{Cite web|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1013 |language=en |title=''Performing Arts: Kagura''|author=Takayama Shigeru|date=February 20, 2007 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto |agency=[[Kokugakuin University]]|access-date=June 7, 2019}}.</ref>
[[File:Urayasu-no-Mai-Kagura.jpg|thumb|{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}}]] *{{transliteration|ja|Miko kagura}} – dances performed by shrine maidens ({{transliteration|ja|[[miko]]}}) originally derived from ritual dances in which the {{transliteration|ja|miko}} channeled the {{transliteration|ja|kami}}, as part of Imperial Court dances. These originally had a very loose form, akin to similar god-possession dances and rituals, but over time they have developed, into highly regular set forms. Today, they are performed by shrines during the {{nihongo||大嘗祭|daijō-sai}} festival and in worship to {{transliteration|ja|kami}} as part of a {{transliteration|ja|[[Japanese festivals|Matsuri]]}}. They are also performed at [[:en:Buddhist temples in Japan|Buddhist temples]] as a [[:en:Martial arts|Martial arts]] performance. These dances are often performed with ritual props, such as bells, bamboo canes, sprigs of {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Cleyera japonica|Cleyera japonica]]}}, or [[:en:Shide (Shinto)|paper streamers]]. *{{transliteration|ja|Izumo-ryū kagura}} – dances based on those performed at [[Izumo-taisha|Izumo Shrine]] serve a number of purposes, including [[ritual purification]], celebration of auspicious days, and the reenactment of folktales. Originally quite popular in the [[:en:Chūgoku region|Chūgoku region]], near [[:en:Izumo|Izumo]], these dances have spread across the country, and have developed over the centuries, becoming more of a secular folk entertainment and less of a formal religious ritual. The sacred dance of the Sada shrine has been inscribed on the [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]] list of [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] since 2011.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-sada-shin-noh-danse-sacree-au-sanctuaire-de-sada-shimane-00412 |title=Le ''Sada shin noh'', danse sacrée au sanctuaire de Sada, Shimane|website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> * {{transliteration|ja|Hayachine Kagura}} – a form of dances derived from [[Yamabushi]] (a mobile group that embraces ascetic lives to gain power). This genre stresses on the power and energy. The performers always wear masks and use tools such as drum and sword to represent the magical power processed by Yamabushi.<ref name=":1" /> It was inscribed in 2009 as an [[:en:Intangible cultural heritage|Intangible cultural heritage of humanity]] by [[:en:UNESCO|UNESCO]].<ref>{{cite web |url=https://ich.unesco.org/fr/RL/le-kagura-dhayachine-00272 |title=Le ''kagura'' d'Hayachine |website= ich.unesco.org |access-date=June 7, 2019}}</ref> *{{transliteration|ja|Yutate kagura}} – a form of dances where {{transliteration|ja|miko}} and priests dip bamboo leaves in hot water and splash the hot water on themselves by shaking the leaves, and then scattering the hot water to people around the area. *{{transliteration|ja|Shishi kagura}} – a form of [[:en:Lion dance|Lion dance]], in which a group of dancers take on the role of the lion ({{transliteration|ja|shishi}}) and parade around the town. The lion mask and costume is seen as, in some ways, embodying the spirit of the lion, and this is a form of folk worship and ritual, as other forms of lion dances are in Japan and elsewhere. *{{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} – a form of dance deriving from rituals performed by traveling priests between [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], who would travel to villages, crossroads, and other locations to help the locals by driving away evil spirits. Acrobatic feats and lion dances played a major role in these rituals.
Around the time of the beginning of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] (1603–1868), performances derived from this emerged in [[:en:Edo|Edo]] as a major form of entertainment. In connection with the celebrations surrounding the beginning of the shogunate, lion dances, acrobatics, juggling, and a great variety of other entertainments were performed on stages across the city, all nominally under the auspices of {{transliteration|ja|daikagura}}. Over the course of the period, these came to be more closely associated with {{transliteration|ja|[[rakugo]]}} storytelling and other forms of popular entertainment. {{transliteration|ja|Daikagura}} continues to be performed to this day and include many elements of street entertainment.
